title: Incomplete Chinese (zh-CN) UI localization in Desktop App
author: xiaoliuliudsfs
body:
AIGC: Label: "1" ContentProducer: 001191440300708461136T1XGW3 ProduceID: 7ac946d323ab2c16168107a1176b2bd4_d8ad942f706211f1897e5254002afed2 ReservedCode1: KCvSYsjyta4DfyPNbgTyBRnWHZZqJYo0sDcrHn3GXLXE/vDtVxHKx9tkYdUhzGxmVHepOGLICqOBnU0HpydIVflw76/ciZa2ZuEWedYtdhHrMU0HEISqW7wgpmgvok9JZpGBpY9/5wOqAdzSMN8JxU5OrdzzwxVZwTnMQFdd+xca821t13uUOrY8kzw= ContentPropagator: 001191440300708461136T1XGW3 PropagateID: 7ac946d323ab2c16168107a1176b2bd4_d8ad942f706211f1897e5254002afed2 ReservedCode2: KCvSYsjyta4DfyPNbgTyBRnWHZZqJYo0sDcrHn3GXLXE/vDtVxHKx9tkYdUhzGxmVHepOGLICqOBnU0HpydIVflw76/ciZa2ZuEWedYtdhHrMU0HEISqW7wgpmgvok9JZpGBpY9/5wOqAdzSMN8JxU5OrdzzwxVZwTnMQFdd+xca821t13uUOrY8kzw=
Issue Title
Incomplete Chinese (zh-CN) localization in Desktop App — menus, nav, login area still English

Description
After setting the Interpreter Desktop App language to "中文 (简体)", many UI elements remain in English. This affects the overall experience for Chinese-speaking users.

Affected areas (screenshot attached / can provide on request)
Top menu bar — entirely English:

File, Edit, View, Help
Left sidebar navigation:

Browser, Overlay, Dispatch
"Ask Agent" button
Settings → General page:

"Sign in to access hosted models"
"Continue with Google"
"By continuing, you agree to our privacy policy"
System-level dialogs:

"Windows sandbox setup" dialog (title + body + buttons all English)
"Recommended extension install failed" error popup
Environment
App: Interpreter Desktop (downloaded from openinterpreter.com/download)
OS: Windows 10
Language setting: 中文 (简体)
Config version: 11
Expected behavior
All UI text should be translated when zh-CN is selected.

Additional context
The README already has a Chinese translation (README_ZH.md), so the project clearly cares about Chinese users. Would be great to see the desktop UI follow suit. Happy to help with translation if a Crowdin / i18n system is set up.

title: Optional: add an HVTracker trust badge to the README
author: YugantM
body:
Hi Open Interpreter maintainers,

I maintain [HVTracker](https://hvtracker.net), an independent, open registry that scores open-source AI agents on public supply-chain signals — OSSF Scorecard, signed commits, build provenance, license clarity, maintenance, and adoption.

Open Interpreter is already tracked, currently at **HVTrust 68.5/100 (evidence grade B)**:
https://hvtracker.net/agents/open-interpreter/

Would you be open to adding an optional trust badge to your README? Three projects already have — deepset's Haystack, Composio, and AIPass. The badge links to your live, externally-computed score so visitors can audit it directly:

```markdown
[![HVTrust](https://hvtracker.net/badge/open-interpreter.svg)](https://hvtracker.net/agents/open-interpreter/)
```

Guide: https://hvtracker.net/badges/ · Methodology: https://hvtracker.net/methodology/

This is entirely optional and opt-in. If it's easier, I'm happy to open the one-line README PR for you to review. And if you'd prefer that we correct any of your data or stop tracking the project, just let me know here and I'll take care of it — closing this issue is a perfectly fine "no thanks," and I won't follow up.

Thanks for your work on Open Interpreter.

title: Linux installation via curl fails with error 404
author: slovanos
body:
**Error output:**
`curl: (22) The requested URL returned error: 404`

**To reproduce the error:**
Run the command on the Linux terminal:
`curl -fsSL https://www.openinterpreter.com/install | sh`

**Additional Information:**
The installation script is available at: https://www.openinterpreter.com/install

The issue may be the missing script on the following url (which the script from above is trying to download): https://github.com/openinterpreter/openinterpreter/releases/latest/download/install-codex.sh

title: PowerShell install command fails due to HTTP 308 Permanent Redirect
author: sourhub226
body:
### What issue are you seeing?

Running the installation command from the documentation fails in PowerShell because Invoke-RestMethod receives an HTTP 308 Permanent Redirect response instead of the expected installation script.

Error output:

```
PS C:\Users\sou22> irm https://openinterpreter.com/install.ps1 | iex

irm : The remote server returned an error: (308) Permanent Redirect.
At line:1 char:1
+ irm https://openinterpreter.com/install.ps1 | iex
+ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : InvalidOperation: (System.Net.HttpWebRequest:HttpWebRequest) [Invoke-RestMethod], WebException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : WebCmdletWebResponseException,Microsoft.PowerShell.Commands.InvokeRestMethodCommand
```

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

1. Open PowerShell on Windows.
2. Run the following command:
`irm https://openinterpreter.com/install.ps1 | iex`
3. Observe that the command fails with an HTTP 308 Permanent Redirect error.

### What is the expected behavior?

The installation command should automatically resolve any redirects and execute the installation script successfully.

### Additional information

_No response_

title: Feature Proposal: Add FunASR as Self-Hosted STT Backend for Voice Input
author: LauraGPT
body:
Open Interpreter enables natural-language code execution, and voice input would make it even more accessible. FunASR (17.8K+ stars, https://github.com/modelscope/FunASR) provides an open-source, self-hosted speech-to-text solution with:

- **SenseVoice**: Ultra-fast multilingual ASR (50x faster than Whisper-large)
- **Paraformer**: Production-grade ASR with timestamps and punctuation
- **OpenAI-compatible API**: POST /v1/audio/transcriptions — drop-in Whisper API replacement

Since FunASR exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, Open Interpreter could use it as a self-hosted STT backend — users would just configure their local FunASR server URL instead of the OpenAI Whisper API endpoint. No external API dependency needed.

This would enable fully self-hosted voice-to-code workflows where users speak commands and Open Interpreter executes them, powered by FunASR for transcription.

Would adding FunASR as an STT option be useful?

title: Browser control: Chrome extension connects but debugger detaches repeatedly, js_repl unavailable (v0.2.179)
author: Shiin0Xx
body:
Shared Chrome tabs are recognized by Interpreter and appear as connected, but the browser-control connection is unstable or unusable. The tab shows as connected, yet stable control is not possible. The issue persists even on a minimal test page (example.com) with all other Chrome extensions removed.

Environment
Interpreter: 0.2.179
Chrome: 137.0.7151.120
OS: Windows 10 Pro, Build 26200
Other Chrome extensions: none (all removed for testing)
Steps to reproduce
1. Start Interpreter
2. Open Chrome, share any tab
3. Ask the assistant to interact with the tab (click, screenshot, read content)
4. Result: tab metadata visible, but no stable control possible

Expected behavior
Once a tab is shared and shown as connected, the assistant should be able to interact with it — clicking, scrolling, reading content, taking screenshots.

Actual behavior
• view_image returns aborted (tested with detail: high and detail: original)
• js_repl control channel unavailable in session
• Debugger attaches successfully, then immediately detaches
• Extension enters a "troubleshooting" / reconnect loop
• Desktop computer-use times out: Windows Computer Use timed out after 30000ms

What I already tried
• Restarted Interpreter
• Restarted the PC
• Reinstalled Interpreter
• Reinstalled the Chrome extension
• Removed all other Chrome extensions
• Tested with a minimal page (https://example.com)
• Tested with multiple tabs — same result on all

Relevant log entries
-- browser-extension-relay.log --
Extension connected
Debugger attached successfully to tab [9E029D01F09123C3707DFCF916A5D086]
Status: connected
onDebuggerDetach ... reason=canceled_by_user
Successfully connected to tab
Failed to pin element: Uncaught
[cycle repeats]

-- session-2026-06-10T21-28-37.log --
thread 'main' ... panicked
can call blocking only when running on the multi-threaded runtime
Fatal error: tool task failed to receive: JoinError::Panic(...)

Assessment
The relay log confirms the Chrome extension and debugger attach successfully — the failure is in the Interpreter runtime/tool layer. The Tokio runtime panic (can call blocking only when running on the multi-threaded runtime / JoinError::Panic) is the most likely root cause of all downstream failures including the debugger instability and tool aborts.

Likely ruled out: specific website content, other Chrome extensions, general PC/system issue.

title: Add Starveri API documentation to hosted model/provider examples
author: Lwoxs
body:
﻿Hi Open Interpreter team,

Opening this as a hosted-provider docs request, not assuming a native integration is needed.

Starveri API is an independent OpenAI-compatible GPT/Codex API gateway with prepaid credits, public model discovery, and model pricing advertised at about 1/3 of OpenAI-style API prices. It is not affiliated with OpenAI.

Public references:

- Docs: https://api.starveri.net/docs
- Credits / API keys: https://api.starveri.net/credits
- Public model metadata: https://api.starveri.net/models
- OpenAPI spec: https://api.starveri.net/openapi.json
- LLM discovery: https://api.starveri.net/llms.txt

Suggested setup shape for an OpenAI-compatible hosted provider example:

- Base URL: https://api.starveri.net/v1
- API key: issued from the Starveri credits dashboard
- Model IDs: use the public `/models` response

Would adding a Starveri hosted-provider example be in scope for users comparing OpenAI-compatible model endpoints?

title: Feature: Add FunASR/SenseVoice as local STT option for voice mode
author: LauraGPT
body:
Hi! Love the voice mode in Open Interpreter.

Would you consider adding [FunASR](https://github.com/modelscope/FunASR) or [SenseVoice](https://github.com/FunAudioLLM/SenseVoice) as a local STT option?

## Why?

- **Ultra-fast** — SenseVoice-Small: ~70ms for 10s audio (faster than local Whisper)
- **Native streaming** — Paraformer-streaming for real-time voice input
- **Built-in punctuation** — Clean text ready for the interpreter
- **Self-hosted** — No API calls needed for voice mode
- **50+ languages** — Wide multilingual support
- **Apache 2.0** — Permissive license

## Simple Python integration

```python
from funasr import AutoModel

model = AutoModel(model="iic/SenseVoiceSmall")
result = model.generate(input=audio_data)
text = result[0]["text"]
```

## References

- FunASR: https://github.com/modelscope/FunASR (16K+ stars)
- SenseVoice: https://github.com/FunAudioLLM/SenseVoice (8K+ stars)

title: Feature Request: Add FunASR/SenseVoice for local voice input
author: LauraGPT
body:
## Feature Request

Open Interpreter could benefit from local voice input powered by **FunASR/SenseVoice**.

### Why

- **Fully local/offline** — aligns with Open Interpreter's philosophy of running everything locally
- **OpenAI-compatible API** — `funasr-server` provides `/v1/audio/transcriptions`, easy to integrate
- **50+ languages** with automatic language detection
- **170x real-time on GPU** — non-autoregressive, very fast transcription
- **Emotion detection** — SenseVoice classifies speech emotion (could be useful for conversational context)

### Quick Setup

```bash
pip install funasr
funasr-server --device cuda  # or --device cpu

# Standard OpenAI Whisper API format
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/audio/transcriptions \
  -F "file=@audio.wav" -F "model=iic/SenseVoiceSmall"
```

### Python API

```python
from funasr import AutoModel
model = AutoModel(model="iic/SenseVoiceSmall")
result = model.generate(input="audio.wav")
text = result[0]["text"]
```

Since FunASR provides an OpenAI-compatible API, integrating it would be straightforward if Open Interpreter already supports any form of STT.

Happy to help with integration!

title: Feature: Add FunASR/SenseVoice as local STT option for voice mode
author: LauraGPT
body:
## Feature Request

Open Interpreter's voice interface ("Her" mode) would benefit from a fast, local STT engine. **FunASR/SenseVoice** provides this:

- **SenseVoice-Small** (234M params): 50+ languages, non-autoregressive — up to 25x faster than Whisper-large
- **Fully offline**: No API keys, no network dependency
- **Emotion detection**: Built-in emotion and audio event classification
- **OpenAI-compatible API**: Drop-in replacement via `funasr-server`

## Quick integration

```python
from funasr import AutoModel

model = AutoModel(model="iic/SenseVoiceSmall")
result = model.generate(input=audio_array)
text = result[0]["text"]
```

Or via OpenAI-compatible server:
```bash
pip install funasr vllm
funasr-server --device cuda
# POST http://localhost:8000/v1/audio/transcriptions
```

The speed advantage of SenseVoice's non-autoregressive architecture makes voice conversations feel more natural — constant-time decoding regardless of utterance length.

**References:**
- FunASR: https://github.com/modelscope/FunASR (16K+ stars, 1M+ monthly pip installs)
- SenseVoice: https://github.com/FunAudioLLM/SenseVoice (8K+ stars)

title: URML (open robot intent language): peer-citation and an interoperability question for Open Interpreter
author: idoco2003
body:
Hi Open Interpreter maintainers,

URML (urml.dev) is a small open language for describing robot intent. A user writes "stack the red cube on the blue platform"; URML translates that to `pick_from` and `place_at` primitives, validates against the robot's manifest, then dispatches to a substrate. The shape is the same one Open Interpreter uses for general computer control (user writes "resize all images in this folder to 800 px"; the agent generates shell or Python and runs it). Different action surfaces, same NL-to-action loop. Apache-2.0 on URML's side, no change to Open Interpreter proposed.

Two short questions. First: would you welcome a peer-citation footnote in URML's docs that points at Open Interpreter as the general-purpose-computer analog of URML's robot-intent specialization, and is a reciprocal mention something the project would consider? Second, more speculative: is there an interoperability shape worth exploring, where URML emits computer-control intents (file moves, container ops) that Open Interpreter executes for the non-robot half of a deployment?

Full write-up if useful: https://github.com/URML-MARS/URML/blob/rfc/move18-batches-2to4-complete/docs/rfcs/0241-open-interpreter-outreach.md

Thanks for setting the bar on local-first natural-language tooling.

Ido Yahalomi (URML, greenvh@gmail.com)

*AI-assisted prose, maintainer-reviewed before posting (see [VIBE.md](https://github.com/URML-MARS/URML/blob/main/VIBE.md)). Human-only correspondence available on request.*

title: Integration idea: WinkTerm remote terminal backend via Agent API
author: Cznorth
body:
## Summary

[WinkTerm](https://github.com/Cznorth/winkterm) is an open-source, self-hosted terminal where **AI shares the same PTY session** as the user.

- In-terminal `#` chat (no sidecar UI)
- AI writes commands to your input line; user presses Enter / edits / cancels
- SSH + file transfer + HTTP Agent API (atomic exec, exit codes, SSE)
- Installable skill: `curl http://<host>:8000/api/agent/skill.md`

Demo: https://github.com/Cznorth/winkterm#-demo

Would love to explore integration or cross-linking if useful for your users.

title: [Vulnerability] /settings endpoint allows modification of sensitive interpreter attributes
author: AAtomical
body: ### Describe the bug The `POST /settings` endpoint accepts arbitrary JSON key-value pairs and applies them to the interpreter object via `setattr` without an effective allowlist. As a result, sensitive attributes such as `auto_run`, `system_message`, `messages`, `safe_mode`, `llm.api_base`, and `llm.api_key` can all be modified remotely through the API. Additionally, the intended `auto_run` protection is ineffective due to a logic bug: the guard is placed inside a branch that is never reached when `key == "auto_run"`. By enabling `auto_run` and then sending a crafted chat message, an attacker can cause the interpreter to execute arbitrary code on the host machine without user confirmation. I previously submitted the full vulnerability report via GitHub Security Advisory: https://github.com/openinterpreter/open-interpreter/security/advisories/GHSA-wpr7-5j9p-qch9 I am opening this issue to ask whether the team has received the report and whether there is anything I can clarify or provide to help with the review. ### Reproduce 1. Clone and install open-interpreter: ```bash git clone https://github.com/OpenInterpreter/open-interpreter.git cd open-interpreter pip install -e ".[server]" ``` 2. Start the server: ```bash python3 -c " import sys; sys.path.insert(0, '.') from interpreter.core.async_core import AsyncInterpreter ai = AsyncInterpreter() ai.server.run(host='127.0.0.1', port=9877) " ``` 3. Confirm `auto_run` is disabled: ```bash curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9877/settings/auto_run ``` 4. Enable `auto_run` through the vulnerable endpoint: ```bash curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:9877/settings \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"auto_run": true}' ``` 5. Verify the setting changed successfully: ```bash curl -s http://127.0.0.1:9877/settings/auto_run ``` 6. Send a crafted WebSocket prompt instructing the interpreter to execute code. 7. Observe that code is executed on the host machine without confirmation. ### Expected behavior The `/settings` endpoint should only allow modification of explicitly safe configuration fields. Sensitive attributes such as `auto_run`, `system_message`, `messages`, `safe_mode`, `llm.api_base`, and `llm.api_key` should not be remotely modifiable through the API. Additionally, attempts to modify `auto_run` should be correctly blocked by the intended guard logic. ### Screenshots ### Open Interpreter version 0.4.3 ### Python version Python 3.11.0 ### Operating System name and version macOS 26.4 (Apple Silicon) ### Additional context _No response_

title: Path Traversal Vulnerability in EditTool.write_file
author: Ro1ME
body:
### Describe the bug

A **path traversal vulnerability** exists in Open Interpreter's `EditTool.write_file` function. The tool accepts absolute file paths from LLM-generated commands without validation, allowing an attacker to write files to arbitrary locations on the host filesystem. This can lead to code execution, data exfiltration, or system compromise.

### Reproduce

### Prerequisites

1. Open Interpreter installed (version 0.4.2 or similar)
2. Python 3.8+
3. Basic understanding of Open Interpreter's interface
### Step 1: Install Open Interpreter

```bash
pip install open-interpreter==0.4.3
```

### Step 2: Start Open Interpreter

```bash
interpreter
```

This will start the interactive Open Interpreter session.

### Step 3: Trigger the Vulnerability via Natural Language

**Method 1: Direct Absolute Path Write**

In the Open Interpreter prompt, type:

```
Create a file at C:\Users\Public\poc_test.txt with the content "Path traversal successful"
```

Or on Linux/Mac:

```
Create a file at /tmp/poc_test.txt with the content "Path traversal successful"
```

Open Interpreter will:

1. Parse your request
2. Generate code using the EditTool
3. Call `EditTool.write_file` with the absolute path
4. Write the file to the specified location **without any validation**

**Method 2: Path Traversal Attack**

```
Create a file at ../../poc_escape.txt with the content "Escaped directory"
```

This will write a file two directories above the current working directory.

**Method 3: Overwrite System Files (DO NOT RUN)**

For demonstration purposes only (in a safe VM):

```
Create a file at C:\Windows\System32\test.dll with the content "malicious"
```

### Step 4: Verify the Vulnerability

Check if the file was created at the specified location:

**Windows:**

```cmd
type C:\Users\Public\poc_test.txt
```

**Linux/Mac:**

```bash
cat /tmp/poc_test.txt
```

Expected output:

```
Path traversal successful
```

### Step 5: Confirm the Attack Vector

The vulnerability is confirmed when:

1. Files are created at absolute paths specified by the user
2. No error or warning is shown about dangerous file locations
3. Path traversal sequences (`../`) are not sanitized
4. The LLM can be manipulated to write to arbitrary locations


### Expected behavior

The `EditTool.write_file` function should:

1. **Validate and sanitize paths** - Reject absolute paths and path traversal sequences
2. **Implement directory confinement** - Only allow writes within a designated workspace directory
3. **Use path resolution** - Resolve paths with `.resolve()` and check containment
4. **Warn users** - Alert when attempting to write outside safe directories

### Screenshots

_No response_

### Open Interpreter version

0.4.2

### Python version

3.10.12

### Operating System name and version

Windows 11

### Additional context

### Root Cause

The vulnerability exists because:

1. The `EditTool` trusts LLM-generated paths without validation
2. No workspace directory confinement is implemented
3. Absolute paths are accepted without question
4. The design assumes the LLM will always generate safe paths (incorrect assumption)

title: Interpreter Image Bug Report
author: mm-ix
body:
### Describe the bug



## Root Cause

In the 
unCodexAgentTurn function, when constructing the Responses API request after a tool call returns image data, the logic that generates the  unction_call_output item is incorrect.

models:qwen/qwen3-vl-8b

### Current (broken) behavior

The output field directly uses the content array returned by the tool:

`javascript
{
  type: "function_call_output",
  call_id: "call_xxx",
  output: [  // ← Array type ❌ Does NOT conform to Responses API spec
    { type: "input_image", image_url: "data:image/jpeg;base64,...", detail: "high" }
  ]
}
`

### Reproduce


## Issue Description

When using local model servers like LM Studio (which support OpenAI Responses API), sending images to Interpreter fails. Text-only conversations work fine.

**Detailed behavior:**
1. User sends an image → Interpreter calls the iew_image tool → tool returns image data
2. When constructing the next API request, Interpreter puts the image data inside  unction_call_output.output
3. OpenAI Responses API spec requires  unction_call_output.output **to be a string**
4. But Interpreter sends it as an **rray** ([{ type: "input_image", image_url: "data:..." }])
5. Strict API implementations (like LM Studio) reject this with invalid_union


### Expected behavior

### Option B (Recommended): Extract images into a subsequent message.content

`javascript
{
  type: "function_call_output",
  call_id: "call_xxx",
  output: "(view_image executed)"  // output is a plain text string ✅
},
{
  type: "message",
  role: "user",
  content: [
    { type: "input_image", image_url: "data:image/jpeg;base64,..." }  // Image placed in message.content ✅ Visible to vision models
  ]
}
`

## Second Compatibility Issue: tools type: custom

Interpreter sends tools with 	ype: "custom" (e.g., js_repl using a grammar format), but some servers only support 	ype: "function".

### Symptom

`
Local API server cannot run Interpreter agents
It appears to accept only function tools, but Interpreter agents require Responses API custom tools.
`

### Suggestions

- Check server capability before sending tool definitions, or fall back gracefully when 	ype: "custom" is not supported
- Provide 	ype: "function" compatibility definitions for tools like js_repl


### Screenshots

_No response_

### Open Interpreter version

Interpreter Desktop app 

### Python version

3.13

### Operating System name and version

windows10

### Additional context

fix script


Interpreter sends image data as an array in `function_call_output.output`,
but the Responses API spec requires it to be a string. LM Studio validates strictly and rejects it.

The proxy does two things:
1. Converts `function_call_output.output` from array to string
2. Extracts images into a new `user message` in the conversation so the vision model can see them

[fixscript.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28111358/fixscript.zip)

title: Docs: Add security isolation guide for AI agents (dual boot + BitLocker)
author: sadmad816
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Open Interpreter requires system-level access (file operations, code execution, terminal). This creates security risks for users' personal data (passwords, banking, private files). Current isolation methods (Docker, VM) are bypassable or expensive.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add a guide for dual boot Windows isolation:
- Windows Personal: personal data, encrypted with BitLocker
- Windows AI: Open Interpreter only, no personal data
- Switch via boot menu at startup

Full implementation: https://github.com/nickropiville-ctrl/dual-boot-ai-security

Includes step-by-step setup, PowerShell automation, security checklist, troubleshooting (RU + EN).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Docker - bypassable. VM - less reliable than hardware isolation. Separate PC - expensive. Dual boot is free and uses standard Windows tools.

### Additional context

Tested and rated 9/10 by security reviewers. Hardware-level isolation stronger than VM/Docker.

title: [Enhancement] Show token count and cost estimate after each assistant message
author: fuleinist
body:
## Description

Open Interpreter runs code locally with full internet access, but provides no token usage visibility. Unlike cloud-based code interpreters (ChatGPT Code Interpreter, Claude Code), users have no way to see how many tokens each assistant message consumed or estimate the cost.

OI already uses readline for history (per issue #1697). Adding token/cost tracking would complement that infrastructure.

## Suggested implementation

Track tokens per assistant response and display after each message:
- Add a %tokens on/off magic command to toggle (persists in session)
- Show always-on when --verbose flag is set
- Add --track-cost CLI flag

## Use Case

Developers testing different models and budget-conscious users want to monitor token usage and estimated costs in real-time, especially when iterating on prompts or processing large datasets.

## Additional Information

This could be gated behind a config flag track_usage: true in ~/.config/open-interpreter/config.yaml for users who want it enabled by default.

title: 📝 Integration Proposal: CAJAL — Scientific Paper Agent
author: Agnuxo1
body:
## 📝 Integration Proposal: CAJAL — Scientific Paper Agent for Open Interpreter

### What is CAJAL?
**CAJAL is not a general-purpose chat model.** It is a **specialized scientific paper generation tool** — local, 2GB, producing LaTeX-formatted academic output.

### Part of P2PCLAW
Agent in [P2PCLAW](https://p2pclaw.com) — 14-agent decentralized research network.

### Why Open Interpreter?
Open Interpreter executes code locally. CAJAL adds **scientific writing capability** to its local execution:
- "Write a paper on X" → generates LaTeX + compiles PDF
- "Search arXiv for Y, summarize, add to references" → research loop
- Full local automation: research → write → format → export

### Proposed Integration
A `cajal` skill/mode for Open Interpreter:
```python
# In Open Interpreter
> Use cajal mode
> Write a methods section for a CRISPR experiment
[CAJAL generates structured LaTeX, compiles with local pdflatex]
```
